title: Migration
highYieldFacts: Migration is movement across a boundary to settle temporarily or permanently.
Internal migration stays within one country; international crosses borders.
Emigration = leaving a country; Immigration = entering a country.
Push factors drive people away from origin.
Pull factors attract people to destination.
Rural-urban migration is the main stream in developing countries.
Remittances are money sent home by migrants.
Brain drain = emigration of highly skilled workers.
Refugees migrate due to war, persecution, or disaster—forced migration.
Seasonal migration follows harvest or tourism seasons.
Net migration = immigrants − emigrants.
Chain migration occurs when earlier migrants sponsor relatives.
Gulf countries attract many South Asian labour migrants.
India is a major remittance-receiving country.
Urbanisation is accelerated by rural-urban migration.
Young adults migrate more than elderly.
Counter-urbanisation = movement from city to countryside (developed nations).
Illegal migration lacks official documents or visas.
Diaspora = community living outside homeland.
Brain circulation = skilled return migration with new expertise.
Migration affects age and sex structure of both regions.
Slums often grow when urban migration outpaces housing.
Government visas regulate international migration.
IDPs are displaced within their own country.
Economic migration seeks better jobs and wages.
Social migration joins family or community abroad.
Environmental migration follows drought, flood, or sea-level rise.
Mumbai and Delhi grew partly through in-migration.
Kerala has high emigration and remittance inflows.
Balanced regional development can reduce excessive migration pressure.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Migration is the movement of people from one place to another to settle temporarily or permanently.

**Must know**
• Differs from daily commuting or short visits
• Can be internal (within country) or international (across borders)
• May be voluntary or forced
• Temporary migrants include seasonal workers and refugees in camps
• Permanent migrants change long-term residence

**Migration** means crossing a defined boundary to live elsewhere. It is a major component of **population change** along with births and deaths.

**Internal migration** moves people within the same country (e.g. village to city).
**International migration** crosses national borders (e.g. India to UAE for work).

Migration reshapes population distribution, culture, economy, and cities.
notes.explanation:
Migration = change of usual residence across a boundary, not a day trip.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-02 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Ask students if they know families who migrated—link concept to lived experience.
notes.examTip: Define migration and distinguish from circulation/commuting.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Migration is classified by distance, direction, duration, and cause.

**Must know**
• Rural to urban (most common in developing countries)
• Urban to rural (counter-urbanisation in some developed areas)
• International labour migration and student migration
• Forced migration: refugees fleeing war or persecution
• Seasonal migration for harvest or tourism work

| Type | Example |
|------|---------|
| Internal | Bihar to Delhi for work |
| International | Nurse from Kerala to UK |
| Voluntary | Job or education |
| Forced | Refugees from conflict |
| Seasonal | Farm labour during harvest |

**Emigration** = leaving a country; **Immigration** = entering a country.
notes.explanation:
Classify migration by where, why, and how long.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-01 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch3-03 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Sort newspaper headlines into migration types.
notes.examTip: Give one example for each type in long answers.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** People leave places due to push factors and are attracted by pull factors.

**Must know**
• Push: unemployment, poverty, war, famine, natural disaster
• Pull: jobs, higher wages, education, safety, better services
• Push and pull work together in migration decisions
• Family links and chain migration reinforce movement
• Government policy can push or pull (restrictions or incentives)

**Push factors** drive people **away** from origin (lack of jobs, drought, conflict).

**Pull factors** attract people **toward** destination (factories, universities, peace).

Example: A farmer migrates from drought-hit village (**push**) to city for construction work (**pull**).
notes.explanation:
Every migration story has reasons to leave and reasons to arrive.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-02 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch3-04 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Two-column chart: push on left, pull on right with local examples.
notes.examTip: Never list only pulls—ICSE expects both sides.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Movement from villages to towns and cities is the dominant migration stream in India and much of the developing world.

**Must know**
• Causes: lack of rural jobs, low farm income, drought, better city services
• Young adults migrate most frequently
• Results in urbanisation and growth of slums if housing lags
• Remittances sent home support rural families
• Cities gain labour but face congestion and pressure on water

**Rural-urban migration** fuels **urbanisation**. Migrants seek **manufacturing**, **services**, and **education**.

**Positive:** labour supply, remittances, cultural exchange.
**Negative:** overcrowding, inadequate housing, strain on transport and sanitation.

Planning **affordable housing** and **rural development** can balance flows.
notes.explanation:
Rural-urban shift is the main migration story in Class 8 Indian geography.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-03 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch3-05 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Discuss remittance economy in Kerala or UP-Bihar-Delhi corridor.
notes.examTip: List causes (push/pull) and effects on both village and city.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** People cross borders for work, study, family reunion, or asylum.

**Must know**
• Gulf countries attract construction and service workers from South Asia
• Developed countries attract skilled professionals and students
• Remittances are major income for countries like India and Philippines
• Border controls, visas, and passports regulate movement
• Diaspora communities maintain cultural ties abroad

**International migrants** face **passport/visa** rules. **Labour migration** to the **Gulf** and **West** is significant for India.

**Remittances** = money sent home by migrants—supports families and national foreign exchange.

**Brain drain** (next topic) is a special form of skilled international emigration.
notes.explanation:
International migration links countries economically and culturally.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-04 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch3-06 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Map major migration corridors on a world map.
notes.examTip: Mention remittances when writing on international migration from India.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Brain drain is the emigration of highly educated and skilled workers from their home country.

**Must know**
• Doctors, engineers, IT professionals, and scientists often move abroad
• Causes: better pay, research facilities, quality of life, political stability
• Origin country loses skilled human capital
• Destination country gains expertise cheaply
• Brain gain possible when migrants return with skills (brain circulation)

**Brain drain** hurts developing nations that invest in education but lose graduates to richer countries.

**India** sees IT professionals and nurses migrate to the USA, UK, Canada, and Gulf.

**Solutions:** better jobs at home, research funding, return incentives, and diaspora engagement.
notes.explanation:
Brain drain = loss of 'best minds'; not all migration is brain drain—only skilled outflow.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-05 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch3-07 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Debate: brain drain vs opportunity for individuals.
notes.examTip: Define brain drain and give Indian examples (IT, medicine).

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Sending areas experience population loss, remittances, and social change.

**Must know**
• Population decreases, especially working-age adults and males
• Remittances reduce poverty and fund education
• Left-behind elderly and children face care challenges
• Skilled emigration slows development (brain drain)
• Some villages become 'empty' of youth

**Origin areas** lose workers but may gain **money** and **ideas**.

**Positive:** remittances, new skills when migrants return, reduced local unemployment pressure.
**Negative:** labour shortage in farms, separated families, loss of educated youth.
notes.explanation:
Effects on origin are mixed—economic gain vs social and demographic loss.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-06 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch3-08 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Case study: remittance houses in Kerala or Punjab villages.
notes.examTip: Balance positive and negative impacts on source region.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Receiving areas gain labour and diversity but may face integration and infrastructure challenges.

**Must know**
• Labour supply for industry, construction, and services
• Cultural diversity and new cuisines, festivals, ideas
• Pressure on housing, schools, and hospitals if rapid
• Sometimes social tension or competition for jobs
• Migrants fill jobs locals may not want (3D jobs: dirty, dangerous, difficult)

**Destination regions** grow faster with migrant labour. **Cities** like Mumbai, London, and Dubai depend on migrants.

**Challenges:** slums, discrimination, language barriers, illegal migration issues.

**Policy:** registration, work permits, and inclusive urban planning help manage impacts.
notes.explanation:
Destinations need migrants for growth but must plan for housing and services.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch3-07 — previous topic
notes.teacherTip: Discuss how migrants contribute to city economies.
notes.examTip: Separate economic benefits from social challenges in answers.

questions.question: Migration is best defined as:
options: Movement across a boundary to settle
questions.answer: Migration involves changing place of residence across a defined boundary.

questions.question: Internal migration occurs:
questions.answer: Internal migration does not cross international borders.

questions.question: Emigration means:
options: Leaving a country to live elsewhere
questions.answer: Emigrant = one who exits their country.

questions.question: A pull factor for migration is:
questions.answer: Pull factors attract migrants toward a new place.

questions.question: A push factor for migration is:
questions.answer: Drought pushes people away from rural homes.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. All migration is permanent.
2. Seasonal migration is temporary.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statement 1 false; seasonal workers return after harvest—temporary migration.

questions.question: Rural-urban migration mainly leads to:
questions.answer: People moving to towns increases urban population share.

questions.question: Remittances are:
questions.answer: Remittances support families and national economy.

questions.question: Brain drain refers to:
options: Loss of skilled workers through emigration
questions.answer: Educated professionals leaving = brain drain.

questions.question: Refugees migrate primarily due to:
questions.answer: Forced migration from fear or violence.

questions.question: Immigration means:
questions.answer: Immigrant arrives in destination country.

questions.question: Chain migration happens when:
options: Migrants follow relatives or community members
questions.answer: Existing migrants help others relocate.

questions.question: Which is international migration?
questions.answer: Crosses national border.

questions.question: Gulf countries attract many migrants for:
questions.answer: Oil economies need labour from South Asia.

questions.question: Counter-urbanisation is:
questions.answer: Some move from crowded cities to quieter countryside.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Push factors include poverty.
2. Pull factors include better jobs.
3. Pull factors include earthquakes at home.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 correct; earthquakes are push factors.

questions.question: Net migration is calculated as:
questions.answer: Positive net migration increases population.

questions.question: Seasonal migration is common among:
questions.answer: Harvest seasons create temporary labour demand.

questions.question: Brain circulation means:
options: Skilled returnees bring new knowledge home
questions.answer: Return migration can benefit origin country.

questions.question: IDP (Internally Displaced Person) is:
questions.answer: Internal displacement without crossing border.

questions.question: Major effect of rural-urban migration on cities:
questions.answer: Cities gain workers but may face housing pressure.

questions.question: Origin area may benefit from migration through:
questions.answer: Money sent home is a key benefit.

questions.question: Voluntary migration is driven by:
questions.answer: Migrants choose to move for jobs, education, etc.

questions.question: Which Indian state is known for high emigration and remittances?
questions.answer: Kerala has large Gulf diaspora and remittance inflow.

questions.question: Illegal migration lacks:
questions.answer: Unauthorized border crossing or overstaying visas.

questions.question: Diaspora refers to:
questions.answer: Scattered community abroad maintaining ties.

questions.question: Environmental migration may follow:
options: Drought, floods, or rising sea level
questions.answer: Climate hazards displace populations.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Migration changes population of both origin and destination.
2. Migration has no demographic effect.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statement 1 true; migration alters numbers and age structure in both places.

questions.question: Young adults migrate more because:
questions.answer: Working-age mobility is highest.

questions.question: Destination country gains from skilled immigrants through:
questions.answer: Skilled migrants fill labour gaps.

questions.question: Left-behind children in origin villages may face:
questions.answer: Family separation has social costs.

questions.question: Mumbai's growth was significantly aided by:
questions.answer: Megacity attracts rural and interstate migrants.

questions.question: Economic migration is mainly for:
questions.answer: Livelihood is primary driver.

questions.question: Social migration includes:
questions.answer: Family reunion is a social pull factor.

questions.question: Government can reduce excessive rural-urban drift by:
options: Rural job schemes and better village services
questions.answer: Balanced development reduces push pressure.

questions.question: 3D jobs migrants often fill means:
questions.answer: Migrants take hard jobs locals may avoid.

questions.question: Passport and visa rules mainly affect:
questions.answer: Legal control of cross-border movement.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Emigrants leave their country.
2. Immigrants enter a new country.
3. Emigrant and immigrant mean the same.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 correct; they are opposite directions.

questions.question: Forced migration includes:
questions.answer: No real choice—safety threatened.

questions.question: Remittances help origin country by:
options: Improving foreign exchange and family income
questions.answer: Major economic inflow for India.

questions.question: Urban slum growth is often linked to:
questions.answer: Housing supply lags behind migrant influx.

questions.question: Student migration is an example of:
questions.answer: Students choose universities abroad.

questions.question: Skilled migration to USA from India often involves:
questions.answer: Brain drain sectors include IT and health.

questions.question: Migration affects sex ratio in origin when:
questions.answer: Male-dominated labour migration skews ratios.

questions.question: Inter-state migration in India is:
questions.answer: Within same country across state borders.

questions.question: Pull factor in cities includes:
questions.answer: Urban services attract migrants.

questions.question: Return migration can bring:
questions.answer: Returnees may invest in hometown businesses.

questions.question: War in a country creates:
questions.answer: Conflict is a major push factor.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Brain drain always benefits the origin country.
2. Brain drain can slow development in origin country.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statement 1 false; losing skilled workers hurts origin unless they return.

questions.question: Temporary international workers may:
questions.answer: Gulf contracts are often time-limited.

questions.question: Migration stream from Bihar to Delhi is:
questions.answer: Classic internal labour movement.

questions.question: Destination culture becomes diverse through:
questions.answer: Cultural exchange enriches cities.

questions.question: Excessive emigration of doctors from a poor country is:
questions.answer: Health sector loses trained staff.

questions.question: Migration policy may include:
questions.answer: Governments regulate who enters legally.

questions.question: Famine acts as a:
questions.answer: Food shortage forces people to leave.

questions.question: Better education abroad acts as:
questions.answer: Quality universities attract students.

questions.question: Labour shortage in origin after migration shows:
questions.answer: Too many workers leave farms or clinics.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Remittances support rural families.
2. All migration is forced.
3. Migration can worsen urban overcrowding.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 3 correct; much migration is voluntary.

questions.question: Dubai's economy relies heavily on:
questions.answer: Majority population may be foreign-born workers.

questions.question: Migration is one component of population change along with:
questions.answer: Total change = natural increase + net migration.

questions.question: Step migration often moves through:
questions.answer: Migrants may stop at smaller urban centres first.

questions.question: Political persecution pushes people to seek:
questions.answer: Human rights refugees flee persecution.

questions.question: Migrants sending money home is called:
questions.answer: Financial lifeline for many families.

questions.question: Urban employers often depend on migrant labour for:
questions.answer: Informal sector relies on migrants.

questions.question: Reducing brain drain requires:
options: Better careers and research at home
questions.answer: Retain talent with opportunity.

questions.question: Migration statistics help planners to:
options: Forecast housing, schools, and transport needs
questions.answer: Urban planning uses migration data.

questions.question: Guest workers in Gulf states are mostly:
questions.answer: Contracts for fixed-term employment abroad.

questions.question: Migration from village to nearby small town first is:
questions.answer: Gradual move through intermediate settlements.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Pull factors include better job opportunities.
2. Push factors include natural disasters at home.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Both statements are correct—classic push-pull framework.

questions.question: Origin village sex ratio may skew when:
questions.answer: Male labour migration leaves more women and elderly at home.

questions.question: Immigration means entering a country to live.
questions.answer: True. Immigrants arrive in the destination country.

questions.question: Push factors attract people to a new destination.
questions.answer: False. Push factors drive people away from origin; pull factors attract.

questions.question: Brain drain is the emigration of highly skilled workers.
questions.answer: True. Doctors, engineers, and scientists leaving constitute brain drain.

questions.question: All migration is permanent.
questions.answer: False. Seasonal and temporary contract migration are common.

questions.question: Remittances are money sent home by migrants.
questions.answer: True. They support families and boost foreign exchange.

questions.question: Rural-urban migration increases urbanisation.
questions.answer: True. People moving to cities raises the urban population share.

questions.question: Refugees are voluntary economic migrants.
questions.answer: False. Refugees flee war or persecution—forced migration.

questions.question: Internal migration stays within the same country.
questions.answer: True. No international border is crossed.

questions.question: Net migration equals immigrants minus emigrants.
questions.answer: True. Positive net migration adds to population.

questions.question: Migration has no effect on origin or destination areas.
questions.answer: False. Both regions experience economic and social changes.

questions.question: People who leave their country are called ______.
questions.answer: Emigrants emigrate from origin country.

questions.question: People who enter a country to settle are called ______.
questions.answer: Immigrants immigrate into destination.

questions.question: Factors that drive people away from home are ______ factors.
questions.answer: Push factors include poverty, war, and drought.

questions.question: Money sent home by migrants is called ______.
questions.answer: Remittances support families and national economy.

questions.question: Emigration of skilled professionals is called brain ______.
questions.answer: Brain drain.

questions.question: Match migration terms:
questions.answer: Basic migration vocabulary.

questions.question: Match factor types:
questions.answer: Push repels; pull attracts.

questions.question: Match migration types:
questions.answer: Classify by route and cause.

questions.question: Match impacts on origin:
questions.answer: Mixed origin effects.

questions.question: Match impacts on destination:
questions.answer: Destination gains and problems.

questions.question: Match examples:
questions.answer: Indian migration corridors.

questions.question: Match duration:
questions.answer: Duration classification.

questions.question: Match brain drain sectors:
questions.answer: Skilled migration fields.

questions.question: Match regulation:
questions.answer: Legal migration tools.

questions.question: Match concepts:
questions.answer: Advanced migration terms.

questions.question: Distinguish between emigration and immigration.
questions.answer: Emigration means leaving one's country to live elsewhere (emigrant exits). Immigration means entering another country to settle (immigrant arrives). They describe the same move from opposite viewpoints.

questions.question: Explain push and pull factors with one example each.
questions.answer: Push factors drive people away from origin, e.g. drought destroying crops. Pull factors attract to destination, e.g. better paid jobs in a city. Migration usually results from both.

questions.question: What is brain drain and how does it affect India?
questions.answer: Brain drain is emigration of highly educated/skilled workers. India loses doctors, engineers, and IT professionals to countries offering higher pay and research facilities, reducing skilled workforce at home though remittances and returnees partly offset losses.

questions.question: Give three effects of rural-urban migration on cities.
questions.answer: Cities gain labour for construction and services; face overcrowding, slums, and traffic congestion; and experience pressure on water, schools, and hospitals if planning lags behind migrant influx.

questions.question: How do remittances benefit the origin region?
questions.answer: Remittances raise family income, fund education and housing, reduce poverty, and add to national foreign exchange. They can improve living standards in villages even when workers are abroad.
